request.question: Which cartridge did John Browning design that has a rim that is the same in diameter as the .50 GI?

title: M2 Browning
M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun
Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1
.32 ACP
Remington Model 241
Remington Model 24
.45 ACP
FN Model 1903
Caliber conversion sleeve
.50 GI
sentences: The M2 Machine Gun or Browning .50 Caliber Machine Gun is a heavy machine gun designed toward the end of World War I by John Browning.
Its design is similar to Browning's earlier M1919 Browning machine gun, which was chambered for the .30-06 cartridge.
The M2 uses the much larger and much more powerful .50 BMG cartridge, which was developed alongside and takes its name from the gun itself (BMG standing for "Browning Machine Gun").
It has been referred to as "Ma Deuce", in reference to its M2 nomenclature.
The design has had many specific designations; the official designation for the current infantry type is Browning Machine Gun, Cal.
.50, M2, HB, Flexible.
It is effective against infantry, unarmored or lightly armored vehicles and boats, light fortifications and low-flying aircraft.
The M2 has been produced longer than any other machine gun.

The Colt–Browning M1895, nicknamed "potato digger" because of its unusual operating mechanism, is an air-cooled, belt-fed, gas-operated machine gun that fires from a closed bolt with a cyclic rate of 450 rounds per minute.
Based on a John Browning and Matthew S. Browning design dating to 1889, it was the first successful gas-operated machine gun to enter service.
The Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1 (Or M1) is a variant of the widely popular M1911 handgun, modified for the .50 GI cartridge.
John Browning's 1911 .45 ACP has been used for many cartridges over the past century.
In every instance the cartridge used a bullet with a diameter that was the same or smaller than the original .451".
Both the Desert Eagle and the LAR Grizzly have been offered in cartridges of .50 caliber.
Both are large cartridges requiring a large, heavy pistol.
.32 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), also known as the .32 Automatic is a centerfire pistol cartridge.
It is a semi-rimmed, straight-walled cartridge developed by firearms designer John Browning, initially for use in the FN M1900 semi-automatic pistol.
It was introduced in 1899 by Fabrique Nationale, and is also known as the 7.65×17mmSR Browning or 7.65 mm Browning Short.
The Remington Arms Model 241 Speedmaster, an autoloading rimfire rifle, was manufactured from 1935 to 1951 from a John Browning design.
Remington reports that 107,345 rifles were manufactured in .22 Short or .22 Long Rifle.
A similar rifle is currently being sold by Browning Arms Company (the Browning SA-22 Browning 22 Semi-Auto rifle).
Based on a John Browning design and manufactured from 1922-1935, the Remington model 24 is a semi-automatic rifle chambered in either .22 Short or .22 long rifle.
It is very closely related to the Browning 22 Semi-Auto rifle (the Browning SA-22) which is still in production.
It is a Takedown gun, meaning that the barrel and receiver are easily separated without tools, allowing for a smaller package for transport.
A case deflector (seen in the image here) that hooked into the receiver and clipped onto the trigger guard was available.
The .45 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), or .45 Auto (11.43×23mm) is a handgun cartridge designed by John Browning in 1905, for use in his prototype Colt semi-automatic pistol.
After successful military trials it was adopted, along with the Browning .45 Colt handgun, as the .45 M1911 cartridge.
The handgun was designated M1911 pistol.
The FN Model 1903 (M1903, FN Mle 1903), or Browning No.2 was a self-loading semi-automatic pistol engineered by John Browning and made by Belgian arms manufacturer Fabrique Nationale (FN).
It was introduced in 1903 and fired the 9×20mmSR Browning Long cartridge.
It should not be confused with the US-made Colt Model 1903 Pocket Hammerless (in .32 ACP), nor with the Colt Model 1903 Pocket Hammer (in .38 ACP).

The FN Model 1903 is based on the same mechanical design as the Colt Model 1903 Pocket Hammerless, which Browning sold to both companies (and others as well), but enlarged to handle the more powerful 9mm Browning Long cartridge.
Due to its reliability, accuracy, light weight, and quick reloading, the M1903 was an issued sidearm for many police forces and militaries.
The pistol was initially introduced by FN as the Browning Modèle de Guerre (Browning War Model) or Browning Grand Modèle (Browning Large Model).

A caliber conversion sleeve or adapter sleeve is a device which can be used to non-permanently alter a firearm to allow it to fire a different cartridge than the one it was originally designed to fire.
The different cartridge must be smaller in some dimensions than the original design cartridge.
Alternative names sometimes imply the type of dimensional difference.
A chamber insert may be used for a shorter cartridge of similar base diameter.
A supplemental chamber or cartridge adapter is typically used for a shorter cartridge of reduced diameter.
A cartridge conversion sleeve may include a short barrel of reduced bore diameter.
Shotgun conversion sleeves may be called subgauge inserts, subgauge tubes, or gauge reducers.
Sleeves intended for rifle or handgun cartridges may have rifled barrels.
Additional variations may allow centerfire weapons to fire rimfire ammunition and/or retain autoloading function with the smaller cartridge.
The .50 GI (12.7×23mmRB) pistol cartridge was developed by Vic Tibbets and Alex Zimmermann of Guncrafter Industries.
The .50 GI was introduced at the 2004 SHOT Show alongside the Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1, a variation of the M1911.
The round has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

sentences: The M2 Machine Gun or Browning .50 Caliber Machine Gun is a heavy machine gun designed toward the end of World War I by John Browning.
Its design is similar to Browning's earlier M1919 Browning machine gun, which was chambered for the .30-06 cartridge.
The M2 uses the much larger and much more powerful .50 BMG cartridge, which was developed alongside and takes its name from the gun itself (BMG standing for "Browning Machine Gun").
It has been referred to as "Ma Deuce", in reference to its M2 nomenclature.
The design has had many specific designations; the official designation for the current infantry type is Browning Machine Gun, Cal.
.50, M2, HB, Flexible.
It is effective against infantry, unarmored or lightly armored vehicles and boats, light fortifications and low-flying aircraft.
The M2 has been produced longer than any other machine gun.

The Colt–Browning M1895, nicknamed "potato digger" because of its unusual operating mechanism, is an air-cooled, belt-fed, gas-operated machine gun that fires from a closed bolt with a cyclic rate of 450 rounds per minute.
Based on a John Browning and Matthew S. Browning design dating to 1889, it was the first successful gas-operated machine gun to enter service.
The Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1 (Or M1) is a variant of the widely popular M1911 handgun, modified for the .50 GI cartridge.
John Browning's 1911 .45 ACP has been used for many cartridges over the past century.
In every instance the cartridge used a bullet with a diameter that was the same or smaller than the original .451".
Both the Desert Eagle and the LAR Grizzly have been offered in cartridges of .50 caliber.
Both are large cartridges requiring a large, heavy pistol.
.32 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), also known as the .32 Automatic is a centerfire pistol cartridge.
It is a semi-rimmed, straight-walled cartridge developed by firearms designer John Browning, initially for use in the FN M1900 semi-automatic pistol.
It was introduced in 1899 by Fabrique Nationale, and is also known as the 7.65×17mmSR Browning or 7.65 mm Browning Short.
The Remington Arms Model 241 Speedmaster, an autoloading rimfire rifle, was manufactured from 1935 to 1951 from a John Browning design.
Remington reports that 107,345 rifles were manufactured in .22 Short or .22 Long Rifle.
A similar rifle is currently being sold by Browning Arms Company (the Browning SA-22 Browning 22 Semi-Auto rifle).
Based on a John Browning design and manufactured from 1922-1935, the Remington model 24 is a semi-automatic rifle chambered in either .22 Short or .22 long rifle.
It is very closely related to the Browning 22 Semi-Auto rifle (the Browning SA-22) which is still in production.
It is a Takedown gun, meaning that the barrel and receiver are easily separated without tools, allowing for a smaller package for transport.
A case deflector (seen in the image here) that hooked into the receiver and clipped onto the trigger guard was available.
The .45 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), or .45 Auto (11.43×23mm) is a handgun cartridge designed by John Browning in 1905, for use in his prototype Colt semi-automatic pistol.
After successful military trials it was adopted, along with the Browning .45 Colt handgun, as the .45 M1911 cartridge.
The handgun was designated M1911 pistol.
The FN Model 1903 (M1903, FN Mle 1903), or Browning No.2 was a self-loading semi-automatic pistol engineered by John Browning and made by Belgian arms manufacturer Fabrique Nationale (FN).
It was introduced in 1903 and fired the 9×20mmSR Browning Long cartridge.
It should not be confused with the US-made Colt Model 1903 Pocket Hammerless (in .32 ACP), nor with the Colt Model 1903 Pocket Hammer (in .38 ACP).

The FN Model 1903 is based on the same mechanical design as the Colt Model 1903 Pocket Hammerless, which Browning sold to both companies (and others as well), but enlarged to handle the more powerful 9mm Browning Long cartridge.
Due to its reliability, accuracy, light weight, and quick reloading, the M1903 was an issued sidearm for many police forces and militaries.
The pistol was initially introduced by FN as the Browning Modèle de Guerre (Browning War Model) or Browning Grand Modèle (Browning Large Model).

A caliber conversion sleeve or adapter sleeve is a device which can be used to non-permanently alter a firearm to allow it to fire a different cartridge than the one it was originally designed to fire.
The different cartridge must be smaller in some dimensions than the original design cartridge.
Alternative names sometimes imply the type of dimensional difference.
A chamber insert may be used for a shorter cartridge of similar base diameter.
A supplemental chamber or cartridge adapter is typically used for a shorter cartridge of reduced diameter.
A cartridge conversion sleeve may include a short barrel of reduced bore diameter.
Shotgun conversion sleeves may be called subgauge inserts, subgauge tubes, or gauge reducers.
Sleeves intended for rifle or handgun cartridges may have rifled barrels.
Additional variations may allow centerfire weapons to fire rimfire ammunition and/or retain autoloading function with the smaller cartridge.
The .50 GI (12.7×23mmRB) pistol cartridge was developed by Vic Tibbets and Alex Zimmermann of Guncrafter Industries.
The .50 GI was introduced at the 2004 SHOT Show alongside the Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1, a variation of the M1911.
The round has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP.

response.hop1_query: Which cartridge did John Browning design that has a rim that is the same in diameter as the .50 GI?
hop1_docs: .50 GI | The .50 GI (12.7×23mmRB) pistol cartridge was developed by Vic Tibbets and Alex Zimmermann of Guncrafter Industries.  The .50 GI was introduced at the 2004 SHOT Show alongside the Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1, a variation of the M1911.  The round has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP.

Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1 | The Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1 (Or M1) is a variant of the widely popular M1911 handgun, modified for the .50 GI cartridge.  John Browning's 1911 .45 ACP has been used for many cartridges over the past century.  In every instance the cartridge used a bullet with a diameter that was the same or smaller than the original .451".  Both the Desert Eagle and the LAR Grizzly have been offered in cartridges of .50 caliber.  Both are large cartridges requiring a large, heavy pistol.

M2 Browning | The M2 Machine Gun or Browning .50 Caliber Machine Gun is a heavy machine gun designed toward the end of World War I by John Browning.  Its design is similar to Browning's earlier M1919 Browning machine gun, which was chambered for the .30-06 cartridge.  The M2 uses the much larger and much more powerful .50 BMG cartridge, which was developed alongside and takes its name from the gun itself (BMG standing for "Browning Machine Gun").  It has been referred to as "Ma Deuce", in reference to its M2 nomenclature.  The design has had many specific designations; the official designation for the current infantry type is Browning Machine Gun, Cal.  .50, M2, HB, Flexible.  It is effective against infantry, unarmored or lightly armored vehicles and boats, light fortifications and low-flying aircraft.  The M2 has been produced longer than any other machine gun.

.585 Gehringer | The .585 Gehringer is a wildcat elephant gun cartridge based on the .585 Nyati.  The cartridge retains the .585" bullet diameter but the case has been lengthened and the rim diameter reduced to fit bolt faces designed for cartridges similar to the .416 Rigby.  It was designed over several years by Karl Gehringer, member of the Australian wrestling team, during service in the Australian Armed Forces.

.380 ACP | The .380 ACP (9x17mm) (Automatic Colt Pistol) is a rimless, straight-walled pistol cartridge developed by firearms designer John Moses Browning.  The cartridge headspaces on the mouth of the case.  It was introduced in 1908 by Colt, for use in its new Colt Model 1908 pocket hammerless semi-automatic, and has been a popular self-defense cartridge ever since, seeing wide use in numerous handguns (typically smaller weapons).  Other names for .380 ACP include .380 Auto, 9mm Browning, 9mm Corto, 9mm Kurz, 9mm Short, 9×17mm and 9 mm Browning Court (which is the C.I.P. designation).  It is not to be confused with .38 ACP, 9mm Ultra, 9×18mm Makarov, 9×19mm Parabellum nor the rimmed .380 Long Revolver CF & .380 Rook Rifle cartridges, introduced around 1870.

.32 ACP | .32 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), also known as the .32 Automatic is a centerfire pistol cartridge.  It is a semi-rimmed, straight-walled cartridge developed by firearms designer John Browning, initially for use in the FN M1900 semi-automatic pistol.  It was introduced in 1899 by Fabrique Nationale, and is also known as the 7.65×17mmSR Browning or 7.65 mm Browning Short.

Automatic Colt Pistol | Automatic Colt Pistol (ACP) denotes various John Browning cartridge designs primarily used in Colt and Fabrique Nationale de Herstal semi-automatic pistols.  All of these cartridges are straight-sided and appear similar.  The .25 ACP, .32 ACP, and .38 ACP are semi-rimmed and headspace on the rim, while the rimless .380 ACP and .45 ACP headspace on the mouth of the case.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: .50 GI rim equivalence and John Browning / ACP cartridges (notably .45 ACP)

- From passage [1]: the .50 GI "has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP."  
- From passage [2]: reference to "John Browning's 1911 .45 ACP" (association of Browning with the .45 ACP pistol/cartridge).  
- From passage [7]: the ".45 ACP" is listed among ACP cartridges (family tied to John Browning); also passages [5],[6] state ".380 ACP ... developed by John Moses Browning" and ".32 ACP ... developed by firearms designer John Browning."  
- Ambiguities / missing facts: it is not explicitly stated in these passages that John Browning "designed the .45 ACP" cartridge (could be ambiguity between pistol designer vs. cartridge designer); the numeric rim diameter value is not given here (missing fact). Bridge entity: ".45 ACP" — need confirmation of designer and rim dimension to answer conclusively.
- Next-hop retrieval priorities:
  1. Fetch the ".45 ACP" article — inspect lede/infobox and "History/Design" for designer attribution and rim/case dimensions.  
  2. Fetch the "John Browning" article — inspect sections listing "cartridge designs" or "works" to confirm whether he designed the .45 ACP cartridge.  
  3. Fetch the ".50 GI" article (infobox/lede) to get the numeric rim diameter and its citation.  
  4. If needed, fetch "Automatic Colt Pistol" or "M1911" sections to disambiguate Browning's role (pistol vs. cartridge design).
hop2_docs: .45 Auto Rim | The .45 Auto Rim, "a.k.a. 11.5x23R" is a rimmed cartridge specifically designed to be fired in revolvers originally chambered for the .45 ACP cartridge.

Automatic Colt Pistol | Automatic Colt Pistol (ACP) denotes various John Browning cartridge designs primarily used in Colt and Fabrique Nationale de Herstal semi-automatic pistols.  All of these cartridges are straight-sided and appear similar.  The .25 ACP, .32 ACP, and .38 ACP are semi-rimmed and headspace on the rim, while the rimless .380 ACP and .45 ACP headspace on the mouth of the case.

.50 GI | The .50 GI (12.7×23mmRB) pistol cartridge was developed by Vic Tibbets and Alex Zimmermann of Guncrafter Industries.  The .50 GI was introduced at the 2004 SHOT Show alongside the Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1, a variation of the M1911.  The round has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP.

.45 ACP | The .45 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), or .45 Auto (11.43×23mm) is a handgun cartridge designed by John Browning in 1905, for use in his prototype Colt semi-automatic pistol.  After successful military trials it was adopted, along with the Browning .45 Colt handgun, as the .45 M1911 cartridge.  The handgun was designated M1911 pistol.

.38 ACP | The .38 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol) also known as the .38 Auto was introduced at the turn of the 20th century for the John Browning-designed Colt M1900.  It was first used in Colt's Model 1897 prototype, which he did not produce.  The metric designation for the round is 9×23mmSR (SR—Semi Rimmed) (not to be confused with the modern 9×23mm Winchester or the .380 ACP).

Smith & Wesson Model 625 | The Smith & Wesson (S&W) Model 625, is a six-round single- and double-action revolver chambered for the .45 ACP (also known as .45 Auto) cartridge and .45 Auto Rim.  The 625 in .45 ACP will headspace the cartridge in the chambers without use of moon clips, but since the extractor cannot engage the rimless cartridge, moon clips are needed to facilitate ejection.  Based on S&W's stainless steel N (large) revolver frame, the Model 625, was introduced as the .45 CAL MODEL OF 1988, in 1988 as a limited edition model intended as an International Practical Shooting Confederation (IPSC) commemorative.  It was equipped with a 5-inch (127 mm) barrel with a full-length underlug.

.25 NAA | The .25 NAA was introduced by North American Arms company for their smaller (height: 1/4" less; length: 1/3" less) and lighter (by approx. 25%) .32 ACP Guardian model.  It is a new design based on .32 ACP, but it is a rimless case necked down to accept .251" diameter (.25 ACP) bullets and case is longer than a standard .32 ACP.
response.summary_2: The .45 ACP. .45 ACP was designed by John Browning in 1905 for use in his prototype Colt semi-automatic pistol. The .50 GI has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as the .45 ACP.
